task2.title: Simon's Task 2 (Essay) Writing Method
task2.subtitle: Former IELTS Examiner Simon Corcoran's Proven 4-Paragraph Framework
task2.corePrinciple: Use a clear, simple structure to deliver substantial content — not complex sentence structures. Target: Band 6.5–7.5.

sentence1.template: It is true that... / There is a common belief that... / Many people argue that...
sentence1.tip: Paraphrase the question — never copy it word for word

sentence2.template: In my opinion, while there are some benefits to X, I believe that Y is more beneficial because...
sentence2.tip: State your position clearly here — DO NOT save it for the conclusion

methodA.name: Firstly / Secondly / Finally (Listing Method)
methodA.use: When you have multiple distinct points to cover
methodA.structure: List several points with light development for each

methodB.name: Idea → Explain → Example (Deep Development Method) ⭐
methodB.use: When you have ONE core idea to develop in depth

structure.description: Topic sentence — state your main point directly
Develop through reasoning, cause-effect, concession, or counter-argument (2–3 sentences)
Support with a specific example (personal experience, hypothetical scenario, or factual case)

methodB.simonsQuote: If you only have one idea for that paragraph, you have to explain it in more detail. Always ask yourself 'WHY?' to expand from idea to explanation.

conclusion.rule1: NEVER introduce new content — only summarize what you have already written
conclusion.rule2: Rephrase your introduction's answer using different vocabulary
conclusion.template: In conclusion, while there are valid arguments on both sides, I firmly believe that... because... and...

essayTypes.structure: Intro: Outline both views + give stance | Body 1: Discuss View A | Body 2: Discuss View B (your stance, more fully developed) | Conclusion: Restate stance
Intro: Paraphrase + state position clearly | Body 1: Reason 1 + Explain + Example | Body 2: Reason 2 + Explain + Example | Conclusion: Restate opinion
Intro: Paraphrase problem + preview solutions | Body 1: Analyze causes/consequences | Body 2: Propose solutions + Examples | Conclusion: Summarize
Intro: Paraphrase topic + briefly answer both questions | Body 1: Answer Q1 | Body 2: Answer Q2 | Conclusion: Synthesize both answers

goldenRules.rule: Answer the question directly. Every body paragraph must tie back to the question keywords. Going off-topic = instant score penalty.
Logic must be clear and organized. Simple linkers (firstly, secondly) are perfectly fine — examiners value internal paragraph logic over fancy transition words.
Good ideas + good vocabulary > complex vocabulary alone. Examiners look for topic vocabulary and natural collocations, not rare words used unnaturally.
Accuracy > Complexity. A correct simple sentence is better than an incorrect complex one.
The 10-minute planning phase IS the soul of your essay. Plan your ideas and vocabulary BEFORE writing. Practice each essay type template until it becomes muscle memory.

task1.title: Simon's Task 1 (Academic Charts) Comparison Method

paragraphStructure.keyRule: ABSOLUTELY NO specific data! Only describe the big picture

overviewGoldenRules: Start with: Overall, ... / In summary, ... / It is clear that ...
Describe the 'big picture' — what changed from start to end?
NEVER include specific numbers, percentages, or year data
Mention: overall trends (up/down), highest/lowest, the biggest changes
Cover ALL visuals if there are multiple charts
task1.simonsQuote: To reach Band 7 or higher, you must present a clear overview that summarises the main trends, differences, or stages. Without one, you cannot score above Band 6 in Task Achievement.

groupingStrategies.strategy: Para 3: Compare all lines at the starting point. Para 4: Compare at the end point + special inflection points (peaks, sharp changes). Group by highest vs lowest, fastest vs slowest growth.
Choose 2 groups to compare — never describe each bar individually. Group by time changes or category rankings.
Compare categories across charts — which segments grew/shrunk? Group similar-sized categories together.
Identify extremes (highest, lowest) and patterns (e.g., older = larger). Group rows/columns with similar characteristics.
Establish relationships between charts — never describe each chart in isolation.

ranking: X was the highest, followed by Y, while Z was the lowest
proportion: X was twice as high as Y
X accounted for the largest share

task1.tenseRule: Past data → Past Simple (increased, fell). Future predictions → will / is expected to. No time given → Present Simple. NEVER use: passive (was increased), continuous (was increasing), perfect (has increased).

dont: Try to mention every data point

readingStrategies.title: IELTS Reading Core Question Types Strategy Guide

tfng.name: True / False / Not Given

definitions.criterion: Passage clearly supports ALL parts of the statement

definitions.meaning: Passage has NO clear information to confirm or deny
definitions.criterion: Passage is silent on this point or only partially covers it

steps: Read the statements FIRST. Underline keywords (names, dates, numbers, adjectives, qualifiers like always/only/most).
SCAN for keywords or their synonyms — IELTS heavily uses paraphrasing; don't rely on exact word matches.
Read one sentence before and after the located sentence for full context. Watch for contrast markers: however, although, despite.
Compare MEANING, not WORDS. Ask: Does the passage SUPPORT (TRUE), CONTRADICT (FALSE), or NOT ADDRESS (NOT GIVEN)?
Use NOT GIVEN as a last resort. First try TRUE vs FALSE. If no evidence for either → NOT GIVEN.

traps.description: always, never, all, none, only — often make a statement FALSE when the passage uses softer language (some, most, usually)
If part matches but a key detail is missing → NOT GIVEN. Check EVERY part of the statement.
NEVER use your own knowledge. Base answers strictly on the passage.
Words may match but context can change meaning entirely. Read the FULL context.
some vs. all, few vs. many — a single qualifier changes the answer
Implied but NOT explicitly stated → NOT GIVEN. Only EXPLICIT statements count as TRUE.

headings.name: List of Headings (Matching)
steps: Read the PARAGRAPHS first, NOT the headings! (Reading headings first will bias you toward distractors.)
SKIM each paragraph quickly to grasp the main idea.
Summarize each paragraph in 3-5 words of your own — this forces you to identify the CORE meaning.
Read the heading list and match your summary to the closest heading.
Focus on the MAIN IDEA, not details. A heading summarizing only one detail is a distractor.
Cross off used headings to narrow down choices. Skip difficult paragraphs and return later.
keyInsights: The FIRST and LAST sentences contain the topic sentence 70%+ of the time.
A keyword appearing in a heading does NOT mean it matches. Check if the heading summarizes the OVERALL meaning.
Start with easier paragraphs — fewer remaining options makes tough ones easier.
After However, But, Yet, In contrast — this is often where the REAL point lies.
Headings that only capture a single detail or example are DISTRACTORS.

techniques.example: Scientists conducted a study ↔ A study was carried out
in the last decade ↔ in the past ten years
Because of drought, yields dropped ↔ Crop production declined as a result of drought
70% ↔ more than two-thirds; doubled ↔ twice as high

longSentences.name: Long & Difficult Sentences — Breaking Method

techniques.description: Mark connectors (however/therefore/because), clause markers (which/that/who), and punctuation breaks
Divide long sentences into smaller sense units by identifying clause boundaries
Replace complex modifiers with simpler expressions (e.g., 'the rapid proliferation of' → 'more')
Fill in omitted elements to reconstruct the full meaning

longSentences.flow: Find the MAIN TRUNK (S+V+O) → Identify subordinate clauses → Recognize participle structures → Note logical signal words → Understand linearly without back-reading
